schools.summary: Gill: Aben Ezra thought God spoke this by night, when the lamps were lighted and burning.
Henry: the candlestick was made long before (Exod. 25:31), but now first the lamps were ordered to be lighted, as the other holy things began to be used.

themes: the LORD spake unto Moses
the lamps first ordered to be lighted
the place of God's dwelling not left in darkness

schools.summary: Gill: it was Aaron's work to light the lamps, though it belonged also to his sons; this first time likely done by Aaron himself — an eminent type of Christ, the great High Priest, who lights the lamps in all his golden candlesticks, the churches (Rev. 1:20), from whom they have all their light.

themes: When thou lightest the lamps, the seven lamps shall give light
Aaron a type of Christ the High Priest
the churches lit and fed by their Lord (Rev. 1:20)

schools.summary: Gill: as God dwelt in the most holy place and sacrifices were begun in the court, it was proper the service of the holy place be set on foot — the shewbread set, incense burned, which needed the lamps lit; Aaron did so as commanded.

themes: Aaron did so; he lighted the lamps... as the LORD commanded
the inner service joined to the outer
the whole house of God in full operation

schools.summary: Gill: the candlestick was of beaten gold, made according to the pattern shown Moses in the mount.

Clarke: the candlestick is an emblem of the church of Christ, the oil of the graces and gifts of the Spirit, and the light of those gifts and graces in action among men (Rev. 1:12-20); God builds his church and sends his Spirit to dwell in and sanctify it.

themes: this work of the candlestick was of beaten gold... according unto the pattern
the church the golden candlestick
the oil the graces of the Spirit (Rev. 1:12-20)

schools.summary: Gill: this was given at the same time as the order about the lamps; the Levites, already taken and numbered, were now to be ordained and consecrated to their office, helping Aaron in the rest of the service.

themes: the LORD spake unto Moses
the consecration of the Levites
the worship of God needing consecrated servants

schools.summary: Gill: henceforward they were distinguished from the Israelites, separated and appointed to their work as ministers to the priests; as Aaron took not the priesthood to himself but was called of God, so the Levites did not assume their office but were appointed to it.

themes: Take the Levites from among the children of Israel, and cleanse them
called by God, not self-chosen
calling and cleansing the pattern of ministry

schools.summary: Gill: the cleansing was Aaron's work, a type of Christ, the refiner and purifier of the sons of Levi (Mal. 3:3).
Clarke: the 'water of purifying' is the water of sin (the sin-offering water), made with the ashes of the red heifer, cedar-wood, hyssop, and scarlet, the heifer's blood sprinkled seven times.

themes: Sprinkle water of purifying upon them, and let them shave all their flesh
a cleansing outward and complete
Christ the refiner of the sons of Levi (Mal. 3:3)

schools.summary: Gill: the bullock was of two years old, as the Jewish writers gather.
Wesley: the sin offering was the same offered for the whole congregation, because the Levites came in the stead of all the firstborn, who in a manner represented the whole congregation.

themes: a young bullock... for a burnt offering... another young bullock... for a sin offering
the Levites standing for all the firstborn
both washing and atonement required

schools.summary: Gill: they were brought within, into the court of the tabernacle, where the altar of burnt offering stood.
Keil & Delitzsch: Moses was to cause them to enter the court and to gather the whole assembly of the children of Israel together.

themes: bring the Levites before the tabernacle... gather the whole assembly
the dedication owned by the people
the ministry consecrated publicly

schools.summary: Gill: they were brought before the LORD, to the altar of burnt offering, where the Lord was present to accept the sacrifices.
Keil & Delitzsch: the children of Israel — the tribe-princes in the name of the Israelites — were to lay their hands upon the Levites.

themes: bring the Levites before the LORD... the children of Israel shall put their hands upon the Levites
the people offering the Levites as their gift
a living offering in place of the firstborn

schools.summary: Gill: 'offer' is 'wave,' signifying that the Levites belonged to the Lord of the whole earth, their readiness to serve, and their constant employment in his service, always in motion — that they may execute the service of the LORD.

themes: Aaron shall offer the Levites before the LORD for an offering
waved as belonging wholly to the LORD
a ministry ever in motion in God's service

schools.summary: Gill: the laying-on of hands transferred their sins to the bullocks, a figure of the imputation of sin to Christ, teaching that doctrine and leading the offerers' faith to the sin-bearing and sin-atoning Saviour.
Keil & Delitzsch: the Levites laid their hands on the heads of the bullocks, the one offered for a sin offering and the other for a burnt offering, to make atonement for them.

themes: the Levites shall lay their hands upon the heads of the bullocks
a figure of sin imputed to Christ
the servants of God cleansed by atonement

schools.summary: Gill: after atonement was made, the Levites were presented to Aaron and his sons, to be their ministers and assistants.
Keil & Delitzsch: thou shalt set the Levites before Aaron and his sons, and offer them for an offering unto the LORD.

themes: set the Levites before Aaron, and before his sons... offer them for an offering
atonement first, then service
reconciled, then employed

schools.summary: Gill: the Levites were separated by the sprinkling of the water of purification, the sacrifices, the presenting before the LORD with the people's hands laid on, the waving, and the setting before Aaron and his sons — and so were the LORD's.

themes: Thus shalt thou separate the Levites... and the Levites shall be mine
set apart by all the rites
the servants made God's peculiar possession

schools.summary: Gill: they went not into the sanctuary, where only the priests entered, nor the most holy place, but into the court of the tabernacle.
Ellicott: they went into the court to keep watch, assist the priests at the altar of burnt offering, and take down and set up the tabernacle as needed.

themes: after that shall the Levites go in to do the service
their work in the court, not the holy places
no holy service but by the cleansed

schools.summary: Gill: the emphatic 'given, given,' repeated for confirmation, or because they were twice given — first to the Lord by Israel, then by the Lord to Aaron and his sons.
Ellicott: they were taken instead of all the firstborn that open the womb among the children of Israel.

themes: they are wholly given unto me... instead of the firstborn... have I taken them
given, given, twice given
substitution: one tribe given that the firstborn go free

schools.summary: Gill: all the firstborn of the children of Israel, both man and beast, are God's own.
Clarke: refers to the manner of redeeming the firstborn (Num. 18:16); they were sanctified to God on the day he smote every firstborn in Egypt.

themes: all the firstborn... are mine... on the day that I smote every firstborn in... Egypt I sanctified them
claimed by right of redemption
the redeemed belonging to God

schools.summary: Gill: God gave up his right in the firstborn, and instead took the Levites for his use and service.

themes: I have taken the Levites for all the firstborn of the children of Israel
God's right released, a substitute accepted
the one given that the many go free

schools.summary: Gill: the LORD gave to Aaron and his sons the Levites that were first given to himself.
Ellicott: the appointment of the Levites in the place of the firstborn was a safeguard, that there be no plague among the children of Israel when they approached the sanctuary.

themes: I have given the Levites as a gift to Aaron... that there be no plague among the children of Israel
the few guarding the many
the ministry a mercy to all the people

schools.summary: Gill: Moses, Aaron, and all the congregation, the several parties concerned in the consecration of the Levites. Calvin: the Levites were inaugurated in their proper order, their condition inferior; the sons of Moses were placed in this lower rank, excluded from the priesthood, so the later ingratitude of Levi and of the people was the more inexcusable.

themes: Moses, and Aaron, and all the congregation... did to the Levites according unto all that the LORD commanded
even Moses' line among the common Levites
each his own place by God's ordering

schools.summary: Gill: they were purified from sin by the sprinkling of the sin-water, or water of purification.
Keil & Delitzsch: the Levites were purified, washed their clothes, Aaron offered them as an offering before the LORD, and made atonement for them to cleanse them.

themes: the Levites were purified, and they washed their clothes... Aaron made an atonement for them
cleansed by water and blood
the double cleansing that fits for service

schools.summary: Gill: they went into the court of the tabernacle, having been at its door when the rites of consecration were performed; all done as the LORD had commanded Moses.

themes: after that went the Levites in to do their service... before Aaron, and before his sons
done as the LORD commanded
the Levite ministry orderly established

schools.summary: Gill: at the same time as the instructions for their consecration, the time of the Levites' entrance on their service and of leaving it was fixed.

themes: the LORD spake unto Moses
the time of the Levites' service fixed
the ministry framed with wisdom and mercy

schools.summary: Gill: this is the fixed and settled time for the Levites' service.
Keil & Delitzsch: from twenty-five years old they went in to wait upon the service, harmonised with the thirty of Num. 4:3 as entering earlier to learn and do lighter service before the full burden.

debates.question: Why twenty-five here but thirty in Num. 4:3?

sides.position: The Levite entered at twenty-five to wait, learn, and do lighter service, taking the full heavy burden at thirty

themes: from twenty and five years old... they shall go in to wait upon the service
learning before the full burden
apprenticeship before full charge (Num. 4:3)

schools.summary: Gill: from the age of fifty years they ceased waiting upon the heavy service of the tabernacle, and served no more in that laborious work.

themes: from the age of fifty years they shall cease waiting upon the service
released from the laborious work
the aging servant honourably spared

schools.summary: Gill: he ministered by giving advice, instructing younger Levites, and doing lighter service, keeping the charge of the tabernacle that no stranger or unclean person enter, but doing no heavy service.

themes: shall minister with their brethren... to keep the charge, and shall do no service
the experience of years turned to counsel
service for every season of life
